change_summary: finding_length grew 151→186 (+23%) — "in a localized cone beneath the sub-lunar track" applied per c3 hole 4 recommendation; tldr_evidence grew 278→334 (+20.1%) and tldr_verdict grew 107→169 (+57.9%) — "smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation" rewrite per c3 hole 1 recommendation. detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text also patched per c3 holes 2 and 3 ("two-bulge" → "smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation"; 0.6% → 1.48%) — these landed under the 20% drift threshold so did not trigger. Sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-09T01:10:03Z; obe_skipped=7 (WIN-063/066/043/006/033/028/004 already re-reviewed since audit).
our_argument_summary: O1 (25.82 h) is the principal lunar diurnal constituent — period derived from Doodson 1921. The dome adopts the standard period without deriving it from disc geometry, and its local moon at 2,534 km cannot replicate the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation observed across all ocean basins. Verdict: Self-Contradicted (correct).

relevant_files: raw-text/08-live-power.txt — Live Model Power dashboard rollup that mirrors monitor.py output
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: raw-text/08-live-power.txt lines 212-244 list M2, K1, S2 tidal domains in the live dashboard with explicit predicted/observed/tolerance/source. O1 and N2 are absent from that list. Section TAUTOLOGICAL DOMAINS (line 297) explicitly enumerates M2=12.42h, K1=23.9345h, S2=12.0h — O1/N2 are not part of the verification suite.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no monitoring code exists for O1 in any form (hardcoded, live-fetch, or otherwise).
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None; no monitoring code at all.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The detail_verdict_text claim "no monitor.py entry exists for this tidal constituent at all" is corroborated by raw-text/08-live-power.txt (M2/K1/S2 listed; O1 absent). Re-confirmed in c4 — no change since c3.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: raw-text/08-live-power.txt lists the dashboard's tidal domains explicitly: M2 (line 212), K1 (216), S2 (240). No O1 domain appears. The Tautological Domains enumeration on line 297 also omits O1.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: O1 is a textbook constituent of the Doodson (1921) harmonic expansion, derived from gravitational forcing by a distant moon. The dome adopts the standard 25.82 h value verbatim; no alternative numerical prediction is offered. The aetheric mechanism is a renaming, not a new computation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Doodson 1921 catalogued O1's period (25.819 h) over a century before the dome model existed. The dome's V51.0 "WIN" is the published value re-stated; no derivation, timestamp, or pre-data prediction document exists for it.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation in raw-text or on the dome site connects O1 to dome parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km, sun_altitude=5,733 km, moon_altitude=2,534 km, H(r)). The period is stated as matching observation, not calculated from disc geometry.

c3_hole_status.hole_1_tldr_two_bulge: PATCHED — both tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict now use "smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation" framing; "two-bulge" wording removed. Verified against current data/wins.json:050.tldr_evidence (334 chars) and tldr_verdict (169 chars).
c3_hole_status.hole_2_detail_verdict_two_bulge: PATCHED — detail_verdict_text replaces "not the observed two-bulge global pattern" with "not the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation observed globally." Verified.
c3_hole_status.hole_3_cross_win_equator_pct: PATCHED — detail_evidence now reads "1.48% at the equator" matching WIN-049. Verified by direct computation: (2534/sqrt(10000²+2534²))³ = 1.48%.
c3_hole_status.hole_4_finding_localized_cone: PATCHED — finding now reads "concentrates force in a localized cone beneath the sub-lunar track" per c3 recommendation. Verified.
c3_hole_status.hole_5_section_anchor: No-op as expected. Anchor still resolves to Section 2.2 / tab "selftest" per V6 mapping.

holes_found.description: Implicit Newtonian-falloff assumption is not pre-empted. The detail_evidence cites "tidal force is a localized spike — dropping to 9% of peak at 5,000 km offset and 1.48% at the equator." Both numbers are computed from the (h/d)³ Newtonian tidal-gradient law applied to a moon at altitude 2,534 km. A dome defender will respond: "You computed Newtonian 1/r³ falloff to attack an aetheric mechanism. The dome's aether does not have to obey Newton's inverse-cube law; you have not derived our falloff from our principles, so your 1.48% number is a strawman." This is a clean rhetorical move because (a) the dome explicitly invokes "aether" as the force carrier, (b) the dome never specifies its own falloff law, so any specific number we cite is by-our-rules-not-theirs. The c3 review caught the cross-WIN inconsistency in the percentage but did not flag that we never address the falloff-law-burden-of-proof question. Same issue propagates to WIN-049 (uses identical 9%/1.48% language). Severity moderate not major: the underlying argument is correct (the dome's burden to specify a falloff law that produces O1's smooth latitude profile from a 2,534 km moon is not met), but our text reads as if we are imposing Newton on them rather than asking them to specify any falloff that fits.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one preemptive sentence to detail_evidence after the 1.48% figure: "The dome model has not specified its own aetheric falloff law, so we apply the Newtonian gravitational gradient as a baseline; the burden is on the dome to specify a propagation law that produces O1's observed latitude-dependent diurnal modulation from a moon at 2,534 km — under any reasonable monotonic falloff law, a moon that close concentrates forcing near the sub-lunar track rather than spreading it globally." This makes the falloff-law assumption explicit AND shifts the burden where it belongs. Apply identical preemption to WIN-049 for cross-WIN consistency.

holes_found.description: Lunar declination overstated as "≈ ±28.6°" in detail_evidence. The 28.6° figure is the maximum lunar declination at major standstill (extreme of the 18.6-year nodal cycle). The typical lunar declination range cycles between ±18.3° (minor standstill) and ±28.6° (major standstill); the time-average is ≈ ±23.4° (similar to Earth's axial tilt). A pedantic reader will note that O1's amplitude responds to the full declination cycle, not just to the major-standstill maximum. This does not weaken the underlying argument (any non-zero declination interacting with rotation produces O1) but invites "they cited the wrong number" pushback.
holes_found.recommendation: Change "the moon's declination ≈ ±28.6°" to "the moon's declination cycle (±18.3° to ±28.6° over the 18.6-year nodal cycle)" or simply "the moon's declination relative to the equatorial plane" without numeric pinning. The argument needs no specific declination value; cite the 18.6-year nodal cycle if a number is wanted.

holes_found.description: Doodson constituent count ("389 in the full Doodson expansion") may not be canonically correct. Doodson (1921) catalogued the harmonic development of the tide-generating potential; standard secondary references variously cite 388 or 396 partial tides. "389" is plausibly correct for some truncation but is not the most-cited figure. Cross-WIN check: WIN-049 does not cite a specific count; only WIN-050 does, so this is a single-WIN exposure rather than a propagated cross-WIN inconsistency. Severity minor because the underlying point ("389 is one of many") is unaffected by ±10 in the count.
holes_found.recommendation: Either drop the parenthetical ("It emerges from the Doodson tidal expansion that produces all major tidal constituents.") or change to a hedged form ("~390 in the full Doodson expansion; 62 is a commonly cited practical subset"). If the specific number is wanted, verify against Doodson 1921 directly or against Pugh & Woodworth (2014) Sea-Level Science §3.3.

holes_found.description: Opening sentence of detail_evidence — "O1 (25.82 hours) depends on the moon's orbital motion relative to the rotating Earth" — is generic. A dome defender can read this and reply: "Our moon also has orbital motion relative to the rotating disc; the dome's moon circles once per lunar day, so 25.82 h is exactly what we predict." The specific declination-cycle mechanism that distinguishes O1 from a simple orbital period appears later in the paragraph ("O1 arises from the moon's declination ≈ ±28.6° relative to the equatorial plane producing once-per-day inequality between successive semidiurnal high tides"), but the opening makes the dome's rebuttal too easy. Severity minor because the later sentence does the work.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the opening to specify mechanism up front: "O1 (25.82 hours) is the principal lunar diurnal constituent — it arises from the moon's declination cycle interacting with Earth's rotation (not merely from the lunar orbital period)." This pre-empts the "any moon that circles once per lunar day matches" rebuttal.

stronger_arguments.description: K1 + O1 sum-frequency fingerprint already noted in c3 stronger_arguments — still un-surfaced in WIN-050 prose. Computed: 1/K1 + 1/O1 = 1/23.9345 + 1/25.8193 = 0.08051 cycles/h, period = 12.421 h, which is M2 to 3 decimal places. K1 and O1 are not independent constituents; they are the diurnal-decomposition partners of M2 generated by the moon's declination cycle on a SPHERICAL Earth. The dome claims O1, K1, AND M2 all match — but on a flat disc with a moon at fixed altitude there is no orbital declination cycle that would split M2 into K1+O1 at exactly the right ratio. Surfacing this would convert WIN-050 from a single-period coincidence claim into a tight three-WIN integer-relation claim that no aetheric mechanism can hand-wave.

O1 amplitude latitude profile peaks near ±30° latitude, vanishes at the equator and poles by symmetry — this is a direct fingerprint of the moon's declination cycle interacting with a rotating SPHERICAL Earth. On a flat disc with a moon circling at fixed altitude, no analogous symmetry argument generates this latitude profile. The dome would need to derive the latitude-dependent amplitude curve from disc geometry alone, and demonstrably has not (raw-text contains no such derivation; monitor.py has no O1 entry at all). This argument is qualitatively in the text already ("smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation") but not surfaced as a quantitative fingerprint. Adding the ±30° peak / equator-and-pole nulls would tighten it.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies O1 as a real, discrete tidal frequency in the 25.82-hour band (data/wins.json:050.detail_evidence). It also correctly recognizes — implicitly — that lunar diurnal forcing exists at all, beyond the popular "twice-a-day tides" narrative that many lay readers default to. The dome's decision to enumerate K1, O1, M2, S2, and N2 separately (raw-text/08-live-power.txt:212-244 dashboard rollup, M2/K1/S2; detail_evidence:050 mentions O1) shows the author has at least encountered Doodson-style harmonic decomposition, which is a non-trivial bibliographic step beyond "high tide twice a day."
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Naming a period is bibliography, not prediction. The dome offers no derivation linking O1's 25.82 h to disc parameters (data/wins.json:050.code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome=false). The dome's automation does not include O1 in the live-power dashboard at all (raw-text/08-live-power.txt; M2/K1/S2 only). Adopting Doodson's 1921 catalogue and re-claiming the entries as confirmed predictions fails the dome author's own definition (john09289.github.io/predictions: "a number derived from the model, registered with a timestamp, and subsequently confirmed by independent data") because the value was published a century before the dome existed. And the period-match alone is the weakest possible claim: the diagnostic content of O1 lies in its latitude-dependent amplitude profile (peaks ±30°, nulls equator-and-pole), which the dome's local-moon geometry cannot reproduce under any reasonable aetheric falloff law.

deeper_analysis: WIN-050 has been on the change-driven candidate list since c3 patches landed between May 5 and May 9. The c3 review caught the most damaging issue (diurnal/semidiurnal conflation in TLDRs and detail_verdict_text — a defender screenshot would have been devastating) and the cross-WIN equator-percentage discrepancy. All four c3 actionable holes are confirmed patched in current data. The c4 review focuses on what c3 missed: the implicit-Newtonian-falloff burden-of-proof question, the lunar-declination overstatement, the Doodson constituent count, and the loose opening sentence. None of these is critical or major — the verdict (Self-Contradicted) is correct and the argument structure is sound. The Newtonian-falloff issue is the most interesting because it propagates to WIN-049 (same 9%/1.48% language), so a single-paragraph preemption applied across both WINs would close the rhetorical hole on a whole sub-cluster of tidal evidence.

citations_verified: Doodson (1921) DOI 10.1098/rspa.1921.0088 — paper title: "The Harmonic Development of the Tide-Generating Potential," Proc. Royal Society A. Period O1 = 25.819 h is in the 1921 catalog. Verified.
(2534/sqrt(5000²+2534²))³ = 9.24% — verified by direct computation, matches "9% at 5,000 km offset."
(2534/sqrt(10000²+2534²))³ = 1.48% — verified by direct computation, matches "1.48% at the equator" assuming pole-centered disc with equator at r=10,000 km.
citations_unchecked: "389 in the full Doodson expansion" — number not directly verifiable without Doodson 1921 PDF; standard secondary references typically say 388 or 396. Flagged as minor hole.

"62 is a commonly cited practical subset" — IHO/NOAA reference list standardly cites 37 or 62 constituents depending on truncation; the 62 figure is canonical for the standard tidal harmonic synthesis.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender, granted our six methodological principles, mounts the following rebuttal to the c4 (post-patch) WIN-050. Point one — the falloff law: "The reviewers compute Newtonian 1/r³ falloff and conclude that aetheric forcing from a 2,534 km moon must be sharply localized. But they have not derived our falloff from our principles. Our aether is a continuous medium; its tidal stress propagates through the disc, not through inverse-cube vacuum gravity. Their 1.48% number is a strawman." Point two — the latitude profile: "They demand a smooth latitude-dependent O1 amplitude curve, but they have not computed what our model actually predicts. The disc-aether equations have not been solved at this level; the model is in development. Demanding a closed-form latitude profile from a model still being elaborated is unreasonable." Point three — the Doodson decomposition: "Once you accept that the moon's motion produces tidal stress at multiple harmonic frequencies, the Doodson decomposition applies to ANY mechanism. We do not need to re-derive O1 from disc geometry; we inherit the harmonic decomposition the moment we accept harmonic forcing."
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Defense (1) is the live wire and is exactly the issue called out as the moderate hole above. Add the falloff-law-burden-of-proof sentence to detail_evidence (and propagate to WIN-049). Defense (2) is partially valid — we cannot demand calculations the dome has not attempted — but the burden remains theirs: claiming O1 is matched by their model commits them to producing the latitude profile their model predicts, otherwise the "match" is empty. The detail_verdict_text already makes this point implicitly ("no derivation, no timestamp, no pre-data prediction"); marginal benefit to making it explicit. Defense (3) is the weakest because Doodson decomposition presupposes a distant gravitating moon producing harmonic forcing — its mathematical machinery is tied to specific orbital mechanics. We could add a sentence: "The Doodson harmonic decomposition is not a mechanism-agnostic mathematical identity; its 1921 derivation presupposes a distant gravitating body producing forcing that decomposes naturally into the 388-or-so partial tides Doodson catalogued. The dome cannot inherit Doodson's decomposition without inheriting the underlying distant-moon orbital mechanics."

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Post-c3-patch state: cross-WIN consistency is now CLEAN on the two issues c3 flagged. (a) Diurnal vs semidiurnal framing — WIN-049 (K1 diurnal) and WIN-050 (O1 diurnal) both use "smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation" framing; WIN-045/046 (M2/S2 semidiurnal) and WIN-051 (N2 semidiurnal) correctly use "two-bulge pattern" framing. The split matches the underlying physics. (b) Equator percentage — WIN-049 and WIN-050 both cite 1.48% at the equator and 9% at 5,000 km offset, matching direct computation of (2534/sqrt(R²+2534²))³ at R=10,000 km and R=5,000 km respectively. The 0.6% discrepancy from c3 is gone. (c) Post-hoc paragraph templating — M2/K1/S2 (045/049/046) use the "both predicted and observed values are static constants" sub-template; O1/N2 (050/051) use the "no monitor.py entry exists" sub-template. This split correctly reflects the dashboard's actual content (raw-text/08-live-power.txt:212-244). NEW issue surfaced in c4: the implicit Newtonian-falloff assumption discussed in holes_found is identical in both WIN-049 and WIN-050 detail_evidence — patching one without patching the other would create a cross-WIN asymmetry. Preemption sentence should be applied to both simultaneously.

claims_checked: O1 period = 25.82 h (claimed in tldr_evidence + detail_evidence)
Tidal force drops to 9% at 5,000 km offset (claimed in detail_evidence)
Tidal force drops to 1.48% at the equator (claimed in detail_evidence)
Moon altitude 2,534 km, disc radius 20,015 km (claimed in detail_evidence)
K1+O1 sum frequency = M2 (cross-WIN sanity check)
quantitative_verification.computations: All falloff numbers computed from (h/d)³ where h=2534 km, d=sqrt(x²+h²): at x=5000 km, ratio=9.24%; at x=10000 km, ratio=1.48%; at x=20015 km (disc rim), ratio=0.20%. K1+O1 sum: 1/23.9345 + 1/25.8193 = 0.08051 cycles/h, period = 12.421 h, matches M2=12.4206 h to 4 decimals. O1 period 25.819 h from Doodson 1921 (citation verified). Lunar declination 28.6° corresponds to major-standstill maximum (typical range ±18.3° to ±28.6° over 18.6-yr nodal cycle).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Two minor numerical issues: (a) "389" Doodson constituents — could not verify against the 1921 paper directly; secondary references variously cite 388 or 396, so 389 is plausibly correct but not the most-cited figure. (b) "≈ ±28.6°" lunar declination is the major-standstill maximum, not the typical range; flagged as minor in holes_found. The core falloff calculations and the K1+O1 sum-frequency identity check out exactly.
